title: NMC grants recognition to PGDCC cardiology diploma after two decade wait
summary: NMC has recognised the Post Graduate Diploma in Clinical Cardiology (PGDCC) offered through Indira Gandhi National Open University, ending a 20-year regulatory and legal battle. Experts say the move could help address India’s shortage of cardiology...
thbAction: Position THB's pharma CRM and omnichannel HCP engagement workflows to support launch planning, KAM orchestration, and compliant follow-up across India and the Middle East.
contentHtml:
• Online Bureau
  • ETHealthWorld
  • Published On Apr 29, 2026 at 06:54 PM IST
  • Read by 100 Professionals

New Delhi: In a development aimed at strengthening cardiac care access in underserved regions, the National Medical Commission (NMC) has granted recognition to the Post Graduate Diploma in Clinical Cardiology (PGDCC), redesignated as Clinical Cardio Physician (Non-Invasive) PGDCCP (NI), offered through Indira Gandhi National Open University (IGNOU).

The recognition ends nearly two decades of regulatory and legal efforts to formalise the programme, which was introduced in 2006 to address India’s shortage of cardiology specialists. The announcement was made by the Indian Association of Clinical Cardiologists (IACC).

Advt

Cardiovascular diseases account for nearly 28 per cent of deaths in India, while the availability of trained cardiologists remains limited. Experts estimate that the country has around 5,000–6,000 cardiologists serving a population of over 1.4 billion, resulting in a significant gap in specialist cardiac care.

Dr. Rakesh Gupta, Director & Chief Cardiologist of Echo & Color Doppler Centre, JROP Institute of Echocardiography, informed that the cardiologists shortage is further aggravated by geographic imbalance, with a majority of them concentrated in large urban centres while a large portion of the population resides in smaller towns and rural areas.

The PGDCC programme was designed to bridge this gap by training MBBS doctors in non-invasive cardiology, preventive care and early detection of heart disease. The two-year course equips physicians to diagnose common cardiac conditions, stabilise emergencies and refer patients to higher centres when necessary.Kapil Khanna, National President, Indian Association of Clinical Cardiologists, said, "Delayed diagnosis at the primary care level often leads to higher mortality and increased treatment costs while placing additional pressure on tertiary hospitals."

According to A. K. Agarwal, former Director, School of Health Sciences, IGNOU, more than 1,700 doctors have completed the diploma over the past two decades through training programmes conducted across 77 institutions nationwide.

Advt

With formal recognition now granted, these doctors are expected to become eligible for government recruitment and integration into the public healthcare system, potentially strengthening cardiac care services in underserved regions.

The recognition follows a prolonged legal battle pursued by the Indian Association of Clinical Cardiologists and several stakeholders. Rajesh Rajan, Chairman,Board of Governors of the association, said, "The programme had long sought regulatory approval to enable wider deployment of trained physicians in primary and secondary healthcare settings."Experts say scaling such programmes could help improve early detection of cardiovascular disease, reduce patient overload at tertiary cardiac centres and expand preventive cardiology services across smaller cities and rural India.

  • By Online Bureau ,
  • ETHealthWorld
